highlights.title: Claude Code v2.1.160 Released
highlights.summary: This release adds safety prompts before Claude Code writes to shell startup files like `.zshenv` and `.bash_login`, preventing unintended command execution. The `acceptEdits` mode now also asks for confirmation before modifying build-tool config files like `.npmrc` that could run arbitrary code. These changes give you more control and visibility over sensitive file modifications.

highlights.title: Claude Code v2.1.158: Auto Mode Now on Bedrock, Vertex, and Foundry
highlights.summary: Auto mode — which lets Claude decide how much reasoning to apply per task — is now available for Opus 4.7 and 4.8 on AWS Bedrock, Google Vertex, and Azure Foundry. To enable it, set the environment variable `CLAUDE_CODE_ENABLE_AUTO_MODE=1` in your shell. This is great news for teams using Claude Code through enterprise cloud providers.

highlights.title: Claude Code v2.1.157: Local Plugins Without a Marketplace
highlights.summary: Plugins placed in a `.claude/skills` directory are now automatically loaded by Claude Code — no marketplace installation needed. You can scaffold a new plugin quickly with `claude plugin init <name>`, and autocomplete now works for `/plugin` commands including installed plugin names. This makes it much easier to build and share custom workflows within your project.

highlights.title: Dynamic Workflows in Claude Code
highlights.summary: Anthropic's official blog post introduces Dynamic Workflows, a feature that lets Claude Code adapt its execution plan based on intermediate results rather than following a fixed sequence of steps. This enables more autonomous, multi-step coding tasks where the agent can self-correct and branch based on what it discovers. It's a significant step toward using Claude Code as a true autonomous coding agent.

highlights.title: Claude Code as a Daily Driver: Claude.md, Skills, Subagents, Plugins, and MCPs
highlights.summary: This in-depth guide covers how to make Claude Code your primary development tool by mastering key features like `CLAUDE.md` project instructions, skills/plugins, subagents, and MCP integrations. It walks through practical setups for real-world workflows, making it a great reference for developers who have the basics down and want to go deeper. Bookmark this one for when you're ready to move beyond simple prompts.

highlights.title: Claude Code – Everything You Can Configure That the Docs Don't Tell You
highlights.summary: A developer dug into the Claude Code source code to uncover hidden configuration options and undocumented settings that aren't mentioned in the official docs. This is a goldmine for power users who want to fine-tune behavior, tweak defaults, or understand what's happening under the hood. If you've felt limited by the official documentation, this post is essential reading.

highlights.title: Claude Code Tutorial: Beginner to Advanced in 20 Minutes
highlights.summary: This YouTube video compresses the full Claude Code learning curve into a focused 20-minute walkthrough, covering setup through advanced usage patterns. It's ideal for developers who want a fast, structured introduction without sitting through hours of content. Watch this first if you're just getting started or need a quick refresher on core concepts.

highlights.title: [Bilibili] Honest Review: Testing 32 AI Coding Tools Including Claude Code, Cursor, Copilot and More
highlights.summary: This Chinese video provides hands-on comparisons of 32 AI coding tools including Claude Code, Cursor, GitHub Copilot, Windsurf, and many others, helping you understand where each tool excels. It's a practical guide for developers trying to pick the right tool for their workflow rather than chasing hype. Even if you're committed to Claude Code, seeing the competitive landscape is valuable for understanding its strengths.

tips_of_the_day: Create a `.claude/skills` directory in your project root and drop custom plugin scripts there — as of v2.1.157, Claude Code auto-loads them without any marketplace setup, saving you configuration time.

Before running Claude Code in `acceptEdits` mode on an unfamiliar codebase, check which build-tool config files exist (like `.npmrc`) — the new v2.1.160 safety prompts will alert you before those are modified, but it's good habit to know what's there first.

If you're on AWS Bedrock, Google Vertex, or Azure Foundry, add `CLAUDE_CODE_ENABLE_AUTO_MODE=1` to your environment to unlock auto mode for Opus 4.7/4.8 — it lets the model intelligently scale its reasoning effort to match the complexity of your task.
